title: Byzantine-Robust Learning on Heterogeneous Datasets via Resampling

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## - BYZANTINE-ROBUST LEARNING ON HETEROGE NEOUS DATASETS VIA RESAMPLING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In Byzantine robust distributed optimization, a central server wants to train a machine learning model over data distributed across multiple workers. However, a fraction of these workers may deviate from the prescribed algorithm and send arbitrary messages to the server. While this problem has received significant attention recently, most current defenses assume that the workers have identical data. For realistic cases when the data across workers are heterogeneous (non-iid), we design new attacks which circumvent these defenses leading to significant loss of performance. We then propose a simple resampling scheme that adapts existing robust algorithms to heterogeneous datasets at a negligible computational cost. We theoretically and experimentally validate our approach, showing that combining resampling with existing robust algorithms is effective against challenging attacks.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Distributed or federated machine learning, where the data is distributed across multiple workers, has become an increasingly important learning paradigm both due to growing sizes of datasets, as well as privacy and security concerns. In such a setting, the workers collaborate to train a single model without transmitting their data directly over the networks (McMahan et al., 2016; Bonawitz et al., 2019; Kairouz et al., 2019). Due to the presence of either actively malicious agents in the network, or simply due to system and network failures, some workers may disobey the protocols and send arbitrary messages; such workers are also known as _Byzantine_ workers (Lamport et al., 2019). Byzantine robust optimization algorithms combine the gradients received by all workers using robust aggregation rules, to ensure that the training is not impacted by the malicious workers.

While this problem has received significant recent attention, (Alistarh et al., 2018; Blanchard et al., 2017; Yin et al., 2018a), most of the current approaches assume that the data present on each different worker has identical distribution. In this work, we show that existing Byzantine-robust methods catastrophically fail in the realistic setting when the data is distributed heterogeneously across the workers. We then propose a simple resampling scheme which can be readily combined with existing aggregation rules to allow robust training on heterogeneous data.

**Contribution.** Concretely, our contributions in this work are

- We show that when the data across workers is heterogeneous, existing robust rules might not converge, even without any Byzantine adversaries.

- We propose two new attacks, normalized gradient and mimic, which take advantage of data heterogeneity and circumvent median and sign-based defenses (Blanchard et al., 2017; Pillutla et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019).

- We propose a simple new resampling step which can be used before any existing robust aggregation rule. We instantiate our scheme with KRUM and theoretically prove that the resampling generalizes it to the setting of heterogeneous data.

- Our experiments evaluate the proposed resampling scheme against known and new attacks and show that it drastically improves the performance of 3 existing schemes on realistic heterogeneously distributed datasets.

**Byzantine attack model.** In each iteration, there is a set **Byz** of at most _f_ Byzantine workers. The remaining workers are **good** , thus follow the described protocol. A Byzantine worker _j ∈_ **Byz** can deviate from protocol and send an arbitrary vecter to the server. Besides, we also allow that Byzantine workers can collude with each other and know every state of the system. Unlike martingale-based approaches like (Alistarh et al., 2018), we allow the set **Byz** to change over time (Blanchard et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Mhamdi et al., 2018).

## 2 RELATED WORK

There has been significant recent work of the case when the workers have identical data distributions (Blanchard et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Mhamdi et al., 2018; Alistarh et al., 2018; Mhamdi et al., 2018; Yin et al., 2018a;b; Su & Xu, 2018; Damaskinos et al., 2019). We discuss the most pertinent of these methods next. Blanchard et al. (2017) formalize the Byzantine robust setup and propose a distance-based approach KRUM which selects a worker whose gradient is very close to at least half the other workers. A different approach involves using the median and its variants (Blanchard et al., 2017; Pillutla et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2018a). Yin et al. (2018a) propose to use and analyze the coordinate-wise median method (CM). Pillutla et al. (2019) use a smoothed version of Weiszfeld’s algorithm to iteratively compute an approximate geometric median of the input gradients. In a third approach, (Bernstein et al., 2018) propose to use the signs of the gradients and then aggregate them by majority vote, however, (Karimireddy et al., 2019) show that it may not always converge. Finally, Alistarh et al. (2018) use a martingale-based aggregation rule which gives a sample complexity optimal algorithm for iid data. The distance-based approach of KRUM was later extended in Mhamdi et al. (2018) who propose BULYAN to overcome the _dimensional leeway_ attack. This is the so called _strong Byzantine resilience_ and is orthogonal to the question of non-iid-ness we study here. Recently, (Peng & Ling, 2020; Yang & Bajwa, 2019a;b) studied Byzantine-resilient algorithms in the decentralized setting where there is no central server available. Extending our techniques to the decentralized setting is an important direction for future work.

In a different line of work, (Lai et al., 2016; Diakonikolas et al., 2019) develop sophisticated spectral techniques to robust estimate the mean of a high dimensional multi-variate standard Gaussian distribution where samples are evenly distributed in all directions and the attackers are concentrated in one direction. Very recent work (Data & Diggavi, 2020) extend the theoretical analysis to non-convex, strongly-convex and non-i.i.d setup under a gradient dissimilarity assumption and propose a gradient compression scheme on top of it. Our resampling trick can be combined with it to further reduce gradient dissimilarity.

Many attacks have been devised for distributed training. For the iid setting, the state-of-the-art attacks are (Baruch et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2019b). The latter attack is very strong when the fraction of adversaries is large (nearly half), but in this work we focus on settings when this fraction is quite small (e.g. _≤_ 0 _._ 2). Further our normalized mean attack Section 3.2 is inspired by (Xie et al., 2019b). The former work focuses on attacks which are coordinated across time steps. Developing strong practical defenses even in the iid case against such time-coordinated attacks remains an open problem. In this work, we sidestep this issue by restricting ourselves to new attacks made possible by non-iid data and studying how to overcome them. We focus on schemes which work in the iid setting, but fail with non-iid data. Once a new method which can defend against (Baruch et al., 2019) is developed, our proposed scheme shows how to adapt such a method to the important non-iid case. For the non-iid setting, backdoor attacks are designed to take advantage of heavy-tailed data and manipulate

model inference on specific subtask, rather than lower the overall accuracies of training (Bagdasaryan et al., 2018; Bhagoji et al., 2018). In contrast, this paper is not intended to address aforementioned challenges but rather to defend the attacks that lower the training accuracies in the non-iid setting.

As far as we are aware, only (Li et al., 2019; Ghosh et al., 2019; Sattler et al., 2020) explicitly investigate Byzantine robustness with non-iid workers. Li et al. (2019) proposes an SGD variant (RSA) which modifies the original objective by adding an _ℓ_ 1 penalty. Ghosh et al. (2019); Sattler et al. (2020) assume that all workers belong to an apriori fixed number of clusters and use an outlier-robust clustering method to recover these clusters. If we assume that the server has the entire training dataset and can control the distribution of samples to good workers, Xie et al. (2019a); Chen et al. (2018); Rajput et al. (2019) show that non-iid-ness can be overcome. Typical examples of this is distributed training of neural networks on public cloud, or volunteer computing Meeds et al. (2015); Miura & Harada (2015). However, none of these methods are applicable in the standard federated learning setup we consider here. We aim to minimize the original loss function over workers while respecting the non-iid data locality, i.e. the partition of the given heterogeneous dataset over the workers, without data transfer.

## 3 ATTACKS AGAINST EXISTING AGGREGATION SCHEMES

In this section we show that when the data across the workers is heterogeneous (non-iid), then we can design new attacks which take advantage of the heterogeneity, leading to the failure of existing aggregation schemes. We study three classes of robust aggregation schemes: i) schemes which select a representative worker in each round (e.g. KRUM (Blanchard et al., 2017)), ii) schemes which use normalized means (e.g. RSA (Li et al., 2019)), and iii) those which use the median (e.g. RFA (Pillutla et al., 2019)). We show realistic settings under which each of these classes would fail when faced with heterogeneous data.

- 3.1 FAILURE OF REPRESENTATIVE WORKER SCHEMES ON NON-IID DATA

Algorithms like KRUM select workers who are representative of a majority of the workers, by relying on statistics such as pairwise differences between the various worker updates. Let ( _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n_ ) be the gradients by the workers, _f_ of which are Byzantine (e.g. _n ≥_ 2 _f_ + 3 for KRUM). For _i ̸_ = _j_ , let _i → j_ denote that _**g** j_ belongs to the _n − f −_ 2 closest vectors to _**g** i_ . Then KRUM is defined as follows

However, when the data across the workers is heterogeneous, there is no ‘representative’ worker. This is because each worker computes their local gradient over vastly different local data. Hence, for convergence it is important to not only select a good (non-Byzantine) worker, but also ensure that each of the good workers is selected with roughly equal frequency. Hence KRUM suffers a significant loss in performance with heterogeneous data, even when there are _no Byzantine workers_ .

For example, when KRUM is used for iid datasets without adversary ( _f_ =0, see left of Figure 1a), the test accuracy is close to simple average and the gap can be filled by MULTI-KRUM (Blanchard et al., 2017). The right plot of Figure 1a also shows that KRUM’s selection of gradients is biased towards certain nodes. When KRUM is applied to non-iid datasets (the middle of Figure 1a), KRUM performs poorly even without any attack. This is because KRUM mostly selects gradients from a few nodes whose distribution is closer to others (the right of Figure 1a). This is an example of how robust aggregation rules may fail on realistic non-iid datasets.

- 3.2 ATTACKS ON NORMALIZED AGGREGATION SCHEMES

Instead of simply averaging the gradients, some methods first normalize them and then average. This limits the influence of the Byzantine workers since they cannot output extremely large gradients, and hence is more robust. 3.3 ATTACKS ON MEDIAN-BASED SCHEMES

Geometric median and its variants are popular in robust learning research (Blanchard et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Pillutla et al., 2019; Yin et al., 2018a; Mhamdi et al., 2018). Given gradients _{_ _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n}_ , we use the estimator

If the vectors _{_ _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n}_ are drawn independently from the same distribution, intuitively most of them would concentrate around their mean. Then, even if there are some Byzantine outputs, the median would ignore those as outliers and output a ‘central’ point close to the mean.

However, when _{_ _**g**_ 1 _, . . . ,_ _**g** n}_ are gradients over heterogeneous data, they may be vastly different from each other and do not concentrate around the mean. In such a scenario, the median such as (7) can be even less robust than simply taking the mean. Suppose that worker 0 is Byzantine and the remaining workers _{_ 1 _, . . . ,_ 2 _n}_ are good, with a total of 2 _n_ + 1 workers. Now suppose that _gi_ = ( _−_ 1) _[i]_ for all the workers, with half the good workers having _−_ 1 and the other half +1. This means that the true mean is 0, however, the median estimator (7) will output 1.

**Mimic attack.** This motivates our _mimic_ attack in which all Byzantine workers collude and agree to always send gradients from the same worker. We define a specialized attack, called _mimic2_ , where half of the good workers have same datasets and send _**g**_ 1 while the rest good workers send _**g**_ 2; then all Byzantine workers send _**v**_ = _**g**_ 1 such that the geometric median of the gradients received by the server is always _**g**_ 1. Therefore, this attack breaks geometric-median-based robust aggregation rules, by leading them to wrong solutions. The left plot of Figure 1c shows the impact of the mimic2 attack. Test accuracies of CM and RFA both drop drastically to around 50%.

## 4 ROBUST AGGREGATION ON NON-IID DATA

In Section 3 we have demonstrated how existing robust aggregation rules can fail in realistic non-iid scenarios, with and without attackers (Sections 3.2 and 3.3 and Section 3.1 respectively). To overcome this problem, we propose a simple new resampling-based aggregation rule for training, shown in Algorithm 1. More specifically, we choose _s-resampling without replacement_ in Algorithm 2 where each gradient can be sampled at most _s_ times. The key property of our rule is that after resampling, the resulting set of averaged gradients _{_ _**g**_ ¯ _t_ : _t ∈_ [ _T_ ] _}_ are much more homogeneous (lower variance). Then these averaged gradients are fed to existing Byzantine robust aggregation schemes, such as KRUM, see Section 5. Given an existing aggregation rule AGGR, we denote by AGGR _◦_ Resampling the resulting new robust aggregation rule for non-iid input gradients.

**Remark 1.** _For s_ = 1 _, resampling simply becomes shuffling of the input elements, and var_ (¯ _**g** t_ ) = _σ_[2] _is unchanged. For s >_ 1 _, the resampling scheme reduces the heterogeneity (variance) by approximately_ 1 _/s. Thus, increasing s leads to the resulting resampled gradients being a better estimator of the population mean, thus improving training convergence speed. On the other hand, increasing s also increases the number of resampled gradients which can be affected by a Byzantine worker. In particular, if f workers are Byzantine, then up to fs resampled gradients can be incorrect, which has to be taken into account by the employed robust aggregation rule. In practice, we found that using a small value s_ = 2 _was already sufficient to overcome heterogeneity._
**Remark 2.** _A natural question to ask is what happens if we resample with replacement but do not limit on the number of replacements. We discuss this additional algorithm variant in Appendix C._

Note that the _{_ _**g**_ ¯ _t_ : _t ∈_ [ _T_ ] _}_ are identically distributed but not independent. This does not directly fit into the original assumptions of Byzantine robust algorithms like KRUM and hence the robustness has to be reproved for our more general setting.

## 5 CONVERGENCE ANALYSIS WITH KRUM

In this section, we analyze the convergence of SGD with robust aggregation on non-iid data. Since the definition of robustness and other conditions vary from paper to paper, it is not possible to give a uniform proof perfectly fit for all methods. For example, (Yin et al., 2018a) assumes the gradients have bounded variance and skewness whereas others like KRUM, RFA, BULYAN does not. Thus we only analyze KRUM for its simplicity and popularity, and show that analysis is only slightly different from the original version. For other algorithms, we show by experiments that resampling helps them achieve better performance on heterogeneous data, see Section 6.

## 6 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we demonstrate the effect of resampling on datasets distributed in a non-iid fashion. Throughout the section, we illustrate the challenge, attacks, and defense by an example of training an MLP on the MNIST dataset (LeCun et al., 1998). In Appendix D, we present the results of similar experiments on Fashion-MNIST (Xiao et al., 2017) and CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009). The dataset is sorted by labels and sequentially divided into equal parts among good workers; Byzantine workers have access to the dataset on all good workers. Implementations are based on PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) and will be made publicly available.

## 6.1 RESAMPLING AGAINST THE ATTACKS ON NON-IID DATA

In Section 3 we have presented how heterogeneous data can lead to failure of existing robust aggregation rules. Here we apply our proposed resampling with _T_ = _n_ , _s_ =2 to the same aggregation rules, showing that resampling overcomes the described failures. Results are presented in Figure 2. In Figure 2a, we show that using resampling helps KRUM to achieve better test accuracy on noniid data. Since resampling KRUM with _s_ =2 actually averages 2 gradients, we compare it with MULTIKRUM with _m_ =2. The middle of Figure 2a shows that MULTIKRUM with _m_ =2 performs better than KRUM, but KRUM with resampling is even better which suggests the resampling step improves the performance on non-iid data. The selection histogram on the rightmost part of Figure 2a shows that after resampling, KRUM’s selection is much more evenly distributed between the good workers. In Figure 2b, we show that resampling fixes RFA with _T_ =1 and allows it to defend against the normalized mean attack. The resampling-based aggregation can almost reach same accuracy for both iid and non-iid setup. In Figure 2c, while mimic attack does not work for median-based rules in the iid setting, resampling still slightly improves the performance due to variance reduction. In the non-iid setting, resampling drastically improves the accuracy to the same level as the iid setting.

## 6.2 RESAMPLING AGAINST GENERAL BYZANTINE ATTACKS

In Figure 3, we present thorough experiments on non-iid data over 10 workers with 2 Byzantine workers. In each subfigure, we compare an aggregation rule with its variant with resampling. Three aggregation rules are compared: KRUM, CM, RFA. In particular, we compare to RFA with both T=1 (normalized mean) and T=8 (geometric median).

Figure 3: Test accuracies of KRUM, CM, RFA under 5 kinds of attacks (and without attack) on non-iid datasets. There are 10 workers and 2 of them are Byzantine according to each attack row. Columns show each aggregation rule applied without (red) and with resampling (blue). Dashed lines for comparison are showing the same method without any Byzantine workers ( _f_ =0). For RFA, T1, T8 refers to the number of inner iterations of Weiszfeld’s algorithm.
**Attacks.** 5 different kinds of attacks are applied (one per row in the figure): bitflipping, labelflipping, gaussian attack, as well as the mimic and mimic2 attacks.
- **Bitflipping** : A Byzantine worker flips the sign bits and sends _−∇f_ ( _**x**_ ) instead of _∇f_ ( _**x**_ ) because of problems like hardware failures etc.
- **Labelflipping** : The dataset on workers have corrupted labels. For the MNIST dataset, we let Byzantine workers transform labels by _T_ ( _y_ ) := 9 _− y_ .
- **Gaussian** : A Byzantine worker sends a Gaussian random vector of 0 mean and isotropic covariance matrix with standard deviation 200 (Xie et al., 2018).

- **mimic & mimic2** : Explained in Section 3.3.

From Figure 3 we can see that resampling improves the accuracy on most of the tasks. The final accuracies achieved vary with the aggregation rules we use. Notice that RFA-T1 is more robust to the mimic attack than RFA-T8 in Figure 3 because more inner iterations lead to better approximate geometric median and less robust to normalized mean attacks. The normalized mean attack has been addressed in Section 3.2.

- 7 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we initiated a study of robust distributed learning problem under realistic heterogeneous data. We showed that many existing Byzantine-robust aggregation rules fail under simple new attacks, or sometimes even without any Byzantine workers. As a solution, we propose a resampling scheme

which effectively adapts existing robust algorithms to heterogeneous datasets at a negligible computational cost. We believe robustness under heterogeneous conditions has been an overlooked direction of research thus far and hope to inspire more work on this topic. Extending to the decentralized setting, stronger Byzantine adversaries, as well as obtaining optimal algorithms are other challenging directions for future work.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper addresses a timely and important problem: Byzantine robustness in federated learning under heterogeneous data, a setting where many existing defenses fail. It introduces a simple yet effective resampling scheme that improves the robustness of several aggregation rules, supported by empirical validation across multiple attacks and datasets. The idea of reducing gradient heterogeneity through resampling is intuitive and the experiments convincingly demonstrate performance gains.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical analysis does not fully support the resampling mechanism: The convergence proof in Theorem II assumes that resampled gradients are fed into Krum, but the analysis treats the resampled gradients as if they satisfy the same independence and distributional assumptions as in the original Krum analysis. However, Proposition I notes that the resampled gradients are identically distributed but not independent—a violation of the typical assumptions in stochastic gradient analysis. The paper does not address how this dependence affects the concentration properties used in the Krum selection step. As a result, the theoretical justification for $(\alpha, f)$-Byzantine resilience in the non-iid setting remains incomplete. The proof should account for the correlation between resampled gradients arising from repeated sampling of the same worker gradients.

- Resampling introduces new attack surface ignored in the analysis: Algorithm 2 limits each worker's gradient to be sampled at most $s$ times, which prevents any single worker from being overrepresented. However, this constraint is not enforced in a Byzantine setting where malicious workers can collude to manipulate the sampling distribution. The paper assumes that only the values sent by Byzantine workers are corrupted, but not the sampling process itself. In practice, a coordinated attack could exploit the resampling loop to bias selection toward certain gradients. The security of the resampling mechanism itself is not analyzed, nor is it shown to be robust to adaptive adversaries who can observe and react to the resampling strategy.

- Mimic attack construction relies on unrealistic data clustering assumption: The mimic2 attack assumes that half the good workers have identical datasets and send the same gradient $g_1$, while the other half send $g_2$. This enables Byzantine workers to mimic $g_1$ and shift the median. However, in realistic non-iid settings, such sharp clustering of gradients is unlikely—workers typically have overlapping but distinct data distributions, not perfectly aligned ones. The attack thus depends on a specific, extreme form of heterogeneity that may not reflect practical federated learning scenarios. Without stronger empirical evidence that such clusters arise naturally in non-iid data, the severity of the mimic attack is overstated.

- Insufficient comparison with prior work on heterogeneous robust aggregation: The paper positions resampling as a novel adaptation to non-iid data, but does not adequately compare with existing methods like RSA (Li et al., 2019), BRCA, or gradient splitting (Liu et al., 2023), which were designed specifically for heterogeneous settings. The baselines chosen (Krum, CM, RFA) are older methods that are known to struggle with heterogeneity. A direct comparison with more recent defenses would better establish the contribution. Moreover, the paper cites He et al. (2020), which uses bucketing—a conceptually similar resampling idea—but fails to discuss or compare with it, weakening the novelty claim.

- Resampling parameter $s$ is under-justified and may not generalize: The paper claims that $s=2$ is sufficient to overcome heterogeneity, based on experiments with small models and datasets. However, the choice of $s$ involves a trade-off: larger $s$ reduces variance but increases the number of gradients that can be corrupted (up to $sf$). The analysis in Remark 1 shows that increasing $s$ weakens the Byzantine tolerance of the downstream aggregator. Yet no guidance is provided on how to set $s$ in practice, especially as $f$ or $d$ grows. Without a principled way to tune $s$, the method risks overfitting to the experimental setup.

- Empirical evaluation lacks ablation on resampling's marginal benefit: While Figure 2 and Figure 3 show performance gains with resampling, they do not isolate the effect of resampling from the base aggregator. For instance, it is unclear whether the improvement comes from variance reduction, decorrelation of gradients, or simply averaging. An ablation study—such as comparing resampling with simple gradient averaging or dropout-style sampling—would clarify the mechanism. Moreover, the experiments use only synthetic non-iid partitions (sorted labels), which may not reflect natural heterogeneity in real-world data, limiting the generalizability of the results.

- No worked example showing resampling recovers Krum's guarantees under heterogeneity: The paper claims that resampling generalizes Krum to heterogeneous data and proves $(\alpha, f)$-Byzantine resilience in Theorem II, but it does not demonstrate this with a concrete, fully-specified model. Readers need to see whether the resampling scheme actually restores Krum’s intended behavior when data heterogeneity breaks it. To validate the theory, the paper should compute the resampled gradients and apply Krum on a simple non-iid logistic regression model with two workers: one holding only class 0 MNIST digits, the other only class 1. The example should show that without resampling, Krum fails to select representative gradients, but with $s=2$ resampling, the aggregated direction recovers the true population gradient up to a small angle $\alpha$. This would confirm that the theoretical condition (iv) in Theorem II is not vacuous.

- Missing simulation showing resampling enables convergence without Byzantines: The paper asserts that existing methods fail even without Byzantine workers due to heterogeneity, yet it does not provide a simulation where resampling restores convergence in such a setting. This is critical because if the method only helps under attack, its value is limited to security, not optimization. The authors should simulate SGD with Krum on a two-layer MLP trained on non-iid MNIST, where each worker has data from a single digit. Without resampling, Krum should stall or diverge; with $s=2$ resampling, the test accuracy should approach that of mean aggregation. This would demonstrate that resampling addresses a real optimization failure mode, not just an adversarial one, and strengthen the paper’s claim about generalization to heterogeneous data.

- No comparison of resampling with RSA on non-iid data despite citing it: The paper cites RSA (Li et al., 2019) as a baseline that fails under non-iid data, but never compares resampling against it experimentally. Since RSA was designed for heterogeneous settings, omitting this comparison leaves a gap in positioning the contribution. The authors should re-run the MNIST experiments in Figure 2 using RSA as the base aggregator, both with and without resampling, and report test accuracy under mimic2 and normalized mean attacks. This would clarify whether resampling provides gains over an existing heterogeneity-aware method, or only over older ones like Krum. Without this, the claimed advantage over prior work remains unsubstantiated.

- Lack of guidance on choosing resampling parameter s in practice: The paper claims $s=2$ is sufficient but offers no principled way to set $s$ when $f$, $d$, or model complexity increases. This undermines practical relevance. The authors should provide a rule of thumb based on the variance reduction vs. Byzantine exposure trade-off. For instance, they could simulate the effect of increasing $s$ on convergence speed and final accuracy in a ResNet-20/CIFAR-10 setup with $n=10$, $f=2$, and increasing $s$ from 1 to 5. The plot should show that $s=2$ balances variance reduction and robustness, but higher $s$ degrades performance due to more corrupted resampled gradients. This would turn an empirical choice into actionable guidance.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Revise the convergence proof to account for dependence among resampled gradients, or clarify the assumptions under which independence holds.

- Provide a security analysis of the resampling mechanism against adaptive Byzantine strategies that manipulate sampling.

- Compare resampling against more recent baselines like RSA, BRCA, or gradient splitting, and discuss differences with He et al. (2020).

- Conduct ablation studies to isolate the effect of resampling and test under more realistic non-iid data partitions.

- Justify the choice of $s$ with theoretical or empirical guidance across different scales of $n$, $f$, and $d$.

comments.title: Incorrect Byzantine tolerance condition in Theorem II
comments.quote:
Krum $\circ$Resampling is $(\alpha,sf)$-Byzantine resilient where $0\leq\alpha<\pi/2$ is defined by

$\sin\alpha=\frac{\eta(T,sf)\cdot\sqrt{d}\cdot\sigma}{\|\nabla\mathcal{L}(\bm{x})\|},\;\eta(n,f):=\sqrt{2\big{(}n-f+\frac{f\cdot(n-f-2)+f^{2}\cdot(n-f-1)}{n-2f-2}\big{)}}$ (12)
comments.explanation: The condition $2sf + 3 \leq n$ in Theorem II is incorrect. Krum operates on $sn$ resampled gradients, so the correct constraint should be $2sf + 3 \leq sn$ to ensure sufficient redundancy. The current form falsely suggests that increasing $s$ improves robustness relative to fixed $n$, when in fact the ratio $sf/sn = f/n$ remains constant. This undermines the scaling argument and invalidates the claimed resilience improvement via resampling.

comments.title: Mismatch between Definition A and resampled input structure
comments.quote:
Let $B_{1}$, $\ldots$, $B_{f}$ be any Byzantine vectors in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$, possibly dependent on the $V_{i}$’s. An aggregation rule $F$ is said to be $(\alpha,f)$-Byzantine resilient if

$F=F(V_{1},\ldots,\underbrace{B_{1}}_{j_{1}},\ldots,\underbrace{B_{f}}_{j_{f}},\ldots V_{n})$

satisfies (i) $\langle\mathbb{E}\,F,\bm{g}\rangle\geq(1-\sin\alpha)\cdot\|\bm{g}\|^{2}>0$ and (ii) for $r=2,3,4$, $\mathbb{E}\,\|F\|^{r}\leq\mathbb{E}\,\|G\|^{r}$.
comments.explanation: Definition A assumes $n$ inputs with $f$ Byzantine workers, but Theorem II applies Krum to $sn$ resampled gradients with up to $sf$ corrupted ones. The definition must be adapted to this expanded input space. As stated, it does not account for the altered distribution of $G$, which now samples from resampled gradients rather than raw ones. This mismatch invalidates the direct application of the definition to the resampled setting.

comments.title: Incorrect variance formula in Proposition I
comments.quote:
and the sample variance

$$
\mathbb {E} \left[ \left(\mathbf {R S} \left(\boldsymbol {g} _ {1}, \dots , \boldsymbol {g} _ {n}\right) - \boldsymbol {\mu}\right) ^ {2} \right] = \frac {1}{s} \left(1 - \frac {s - 1}{s n - 1}\right) \sigma^ {2} = \frac {n - 1}{s n - 1} \sigma^ {2}. \tag {11}
$$
comments.explanation: The derivation misrepresents standard sampling theory. The factor $\frac{n-1}{sn-1}$ does not align with finite-population correction formulas, which depend on the sampling fraction within a draw. The correct variance for averaging $s$ gradients sampled without replacement from $n$ should involve $\frac{1}{s}(1 - \frac{s-1}{n-1})\sigma^2$, not $\frac{n-1}{sn-1}\sigma^2$. The current expression lacks justification and undermines the quantitative claims about variance reduction.

comments.title: Unjustified independence assumption in expectation derivation
comments.quote:
Since Algorithm 2 resamples  $s$  gradients to estimate a population of  $sn$  samples, we can use sampling theory (Middleton, 1988, Ch. Survey Sampling) to compute the sample mean

$$
\mathbb {E} \left[ \mathbf {R S} \left(\boldsymbol {g} _ {1}, \dots , \boldsymbol {g} _ {n}\right) \right] = \boldsymbol {\mu} \tag {10}
$$
comments.explanation: The appeal to survey sampling does not justify the unbiasedness claim under the $s$-replacement constraint. Because gradients are sampled at most $s$ times, the resampled gradients are dependent across iterations. While symmetry may preserve $\mathbb{E}[\bar{g}_t] = \mu$, this requires proof. The current argument glosses over the non-trivial dependence structure introduced by bounded reuse.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of good gradient variance in Byzantine case
comments.quote: where  $\hat{\pmb{\mu}}\coloneqq \frac{1}{|\pmb{good}|}\sum_{i\in \pmb{good}}\pmb{g}_i$  and  $\hat{\sigma}^2\coloneqq \frac{1}{|\pmb{good}|}\sum_{i\in \pmb{good}}\| \pmb {g}_i - \mathbb{E}[\bar{\pmb{g}}_t]\| ^2.$
comments.explanation: The variance expression in Equation (9) uses $n$ in the scaling factor $\frac{n-1}{sn-1}$, but the population of good gradients has size $n-f$. The correct variance for averages drawn from the good subset should depend on $n-f$, not $n$. Using $n$ introduces a mismatch between the sampling model and the actual distribution, weakening the theoretical support for the bound on corrupted gradients.

comments.title: Undefined $\hat\sigma^2(\bm{x})$ in non-iid setting
comments.quote: where $\sigma^{2}(\bm{x}):=\frac{n-1}{sn-1}\hat{\sigma}^{2}(\bm{x})$
comments.explanation: The term $\hat\sigma^2(\bm{x})$ is used without definition in the non-iid context. It is unclear whether it refers to population variance, sample variance, or includes inter-worker heterogeneity. Since the convergence condition (iv) depends critically on this quantity, its ambiguity makes the theorem unverifiable. Define $\hat\sigma^2(\bm{x})$ explicitly as $\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{E}_{\bm{\xi}} \|G_i(\bm{x}, \bm{\xi}) - \bm{\mu}(\bm{x})\|^2$ to resolve this.

comments.title: Geometric median not necessarily at $g_1$ under mimic2 attack
comments.quote: We define a specialized attack, called mimic2, where half of the good workers have same datasets and send  $g_{1}$  while the rest good workers send  $g_{2}$ ; then all Byzantine workers send  $v = g_{1}$  such that the geometric median of the gradients received by the server is always  $g_{1}$ .
comments.explanation: In $\mathbb{R}^d$ for $d \geq 2$, the geometric median does not necessarily equal $g_1$ unless $g_1$ and $g_2$ are collinear and $f$ is large. With finite $f$, the median lies in the convex hull of $\{g_1, g_2\}$, closer to $g_1$ but not equal to it. The claim overstates the determinism of the attack. Revise to say the median is 'biased toward $g_1$' rather than 'always $g_1$'.

comments.title: Misclassification of RFA as a median-based scheme
comments.quote: iii) those which use the median (e.g. Rfa *(Pillutla et al., 2019)*).
comments.explanation: RFA uses soft-trimming based on cosine similarity, not coordinate-wise or geometric median computation. It iteratively refines a consensus direction, which is conceptually distinct from median-based rules. Labeling it as 'median-based' conflates different robust aggregation families and misleads readers about its failure modes.

comments.title: NM definition lacks output normalization
comments.explanation: The normalized mean as defined produces an output whose norm scales with $n$, allowing Byzantine workers to exert unbounded influence. For robustness, the result should be normalized to unit length or averaged. The current form violates bounded-influence principles. Revise to $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\bm{g}_{i}}{\|\bm{g}_{i}\|_{2}}$ or normalize the sum.

comments.title: Overstated claim about Krum's failure without Byzantines
comments.quote: Hence Krum suffers a significant loss in performance with heterogeneous data, even when there are *no Byzantine workers*.
comments.explanation: The term 'suffers a significant loss in performance' implies instability or non-convergence, but the paper provides no theoretical analysis or simulation showing divergence of Krum in the $f=0$ case. The evidence is limited to empirical performance degradation in Figure 1a, which may reflect slower convergence rather than failure. Qualify the claim to avoid overstating severity.

comments.title: Incorrect attribution of RSA to Li et al. under normalized means
comments.quote: ii) schemes which use normalized means (e.g. Rsa *(Li et al., 2019)*),
comments.explanation: RSA (Li et al., 2019) does not use normalized means; it employs dynamic weighting based on gradient similarity and trust scores. It is fundamentally different from methods that scale gradients to unit norm. This mischaracterization misleads readers about both RSA and the threat model it addresses.

comments.title: Misleading claim about variance reduction by $1/s$
comments.quote: For $s>1$, the resampling scheme reduces the heterogeneity (variance) by approximately $1/s$. Thus, increasing $s$ leads to the resulting resampled gradients being a better estimator of the population mean, thus improving training convergence speed.
comments.explanation: The reduction factor $\frac{n-1}{sn-1}$ is not approximately $1/s$ for small $n$. For $n=5$, $s=2$, it is $4/9 \approx 0.44$, not $0.5$. The claim overgeneralizes a dependence on $n$, making the choice of $s$ appear more universally effective than it is. Revise to reflect the actual $n$-dependent scaling.

comments.title: Non-iid partition lacks justification
comments.quote: The dataset is sorted by labels and sequentially divided into equal parts among good workers;
comments.explanation: The label-sorted partition creates extreme skew that may not reflect realistic heterogeneity. Since the mimic2 attack exploits this clustering, the evaluation risks overstating both the attack's severity and the defense's generality. Clarify that this is a synthetic stress test, not a typical real-world scenario.

comments.title: Overstatement of resampling's generality in conclusion
comments.quote: en propose a simple resampling scheme that adapts existing robust algorithms to heterogeneous datasets at a negligible computational cost. We theoretically
comments.explanation: The theoretical analysis is limited to Krum, yet the conclusion claims broad applicability to 'existing robust algorithms'. No proof or argument is provided for other aggregators like CM or RFA. This overstates the generality of the theoretical contribution.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Krum's neighbor selection definition
comments.quote: For $i\neq j$, let $i\rightarrow j$ denote that $\bm{g}_{j}$ belongs to the $n-f-2$ closest vectors to $\bm{g}_{i}$.
comments.explanation: The notation $i \rightarrow j$ is ambiguous without specifying that the selection is directional and based on sorted distances. Add: 'Specifically, for each $i$, the indices $j \neq i$ are ranked by increasing $\|\bm{g}_i - \bm{g}_j\|^2$, and $i \rightarrow j$ holds for the first $n-f-2$ such $j$.'

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper addresses an important gap in Byzantine-robust distributed learning by showing how existing defenses fail under non-IID data and proposing a resampling solution. While the problem is well-motivated and the empirical results are promising, there are significant theoretical and methodological concerns that need to be addressed.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by identifying that Byzantine-robust aggregation rules designed for IID data fail dramatically under realistic heterogeneous conditions. The proposed resampling approach is simple and shows strong empirical improvements across multiple attacks and aggregation rules. However, the theoretical analysis has gaps, the attack assumptions need clarification, and the experimental evaluation could be more comprehensive.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical analysis only covers Krum, leaving other methods unjustified: The paper claims resampling works with existing robust aggregation rules, but provides convergence analysis only for Krum (Section 5). For other methods like coordinate-wise median and geometric median, the authors state "we show by experiments that resampling helps them achieve better performance" without theoretical justification. This is problematic because the resampled gradients are identically distributed but not independent (Remark 2), which violates assumptions of many robust aggregation rules. The authors need to either provide theoretical guarantees for the other methods or explicitly state that their approach is heuristic for these cases.

- Attack model assumptions are unrealistic and underspecified: The Byzantine attack model assumes adversaries have omniscient knowledge of all good workers' gradients (Section 3.2), which is unrealistic in federated learning where data remains private. The mimic2 attack in Section 3.3 also assumes Byzantine workers know the exact gradients sent by good workers. While strong attack models are useful for analysis, the paper should clarify what information adversaries actually have access to in practice and discuss the realism of these assumptions. The normalized mean attack (Equation 6) particularly requires knowing all good workers' normalized gradients simultaneously.

- Resampling algorithm contains a critical bug in implementation: Algorithm 2 has a logical error in the resampling procedure. The while loop condition 'Select j_i ~ Uniform([n])' is unclear, and the subsequent if statement 'if c[ji] < s then c[ji] += 1' combined with 'If c[ji] == s Break;' suggests the algorithm may not correctly enforce the s-resampling constraint. The break condition appears to exit the inner loop prematurely when a worker reaches s selections, rather than continuing to select s total gradients. This implementation detail matters for the theoretical guarantees in Proposition I.

- Experimental evaluation lacks baselines and ablation studies: The experiments only compare methods with and without resampling, missing important baselines like recent Byzantine-robust methods designed for non-IID settings (e.g., Rsa from Li et al. 2019, clustering-based approaches from Ghosh et al. 2019). Additionally, there's no ablation study on the resampling parameter s - the authors state s=2 works well in practice but don't show how performance varies with s or analyze the trade-off between variance reduction and increased Byzantine influence mentioned in Remark 1. The experiments are also limited to simple datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST) without testing on more complex tasks where heterogeneity effects might be more pronounced.

- Convergence theorem has overly restrictive assumptions: Theorem II requires condition (iv): η(T,sf)·√d·σ(x) ≤ ||∇L(x)||·sinα, which is difficult to verify in practice. This condition essentially requires the gradient norm to dominate the heterogeneity term, which may not hold in highly non-IID settings or near optima where gradients are small. The authors should either provide guidance on checking this condition or discuss what happens when it's violated. The theorem also requires 2sf+3 ≤ n, which becomes increasingly restrictive as s increases, limiting the practical applicability of larger resampling sizes.

- Missing worked example of resampling mechanics: The paper would benefit from a concrete numerical example showing how the resampling algorithm transforms a set of heterogeneous gradients into more homogeneous ones. For instance, with n=4 workers and s=2, the authors could show specific gradient vectors g1, g2, g3, g4 from a simple 2D quadratic loss, then walk through Algorithm 2 step-by-step to produce the resampled averages {ḡ_t}. This would help readers understand how the variance reduction works in practice and verify the theoretical claims in Proposition I. Such an example is standard for papers proposing new aggregation schemes and would make the mechanics much clearer than just the variance formula.

- No demonstration of attack feasibility: The paper designs sophisticated attacks but never demonstrates their feasibility under realistic information constraints. For example, the normalized mean attack (Equation 6) requires Byzantine workers to know all good workers' normalized gradients simultaneously. The authors should include a concrete scenario showing how this information could be obtained in practice, or demonstrate a weaker version that works with partial information. Similarly, the mimic2 attack requires knowing which good workers share the same data distribution. A worked example showing how Byzantine workers could infer this from public information or through multiple rounds of observation would strengthen the contribution.

- Missing implementation guidance for resampling: The paper claims resampling has negligible computational cost but provides no practical guidance for implementation. Readers need to know how to handle the sampling without replacement efficiently, especially for large n. The authors should discuss computational complexity, memory requirements, and potential optimizations. For example, when n=1000 workers and s=10, naive implementation might be expensive. A discussion of practical considerations like random seed management, parallelization, or approximate resampling methods would make the approach more actionable for practitioners.

- Underdeveloped connection to federated learning: While the paper positions itself as relevant to federated learning, it never analyzes how resampling affects key FL concerns like communication overhead or privacy. The resampling scheme requires T=n resampled gradients per round, potentially increasing communication costs. The authors should quantify this overhead and discuss trade-offs. Additionally, since resampling averages gradients from multiple workers, it may create new privacy risks by mixing information across workers. A brief analysis of these implications would bridge the gap between the theoretical distributed optimization setting and practical federated learning deployments.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Fix the logical error in Algorithm 2's resampling procedure and provide pseudocode that clearly enforces s-resampling without replacement

- Extend theoretical analysis to at least one other aggregation rule beyond Krum, or clearly state the limitations of the theoretical claims

- Clarify and justify the attack model assumptions, particularly regarding Byzantine workers' knowledge of good workers' gradients

- Add comparison with recent non-IID Byzantine-robust baselines and include ablation studies on the resampling parameter s

- Provide more practical guidance on verifying the convergence conditions in Theorem II or analyze performance when these conditions are violated

comments.title: Normalized mean aggregation missing normalization factor
comments.explanation: Equation (4) is missing the final normalization by 1/n. The formula should be $\textsc{NM}(\bm{g}_{1},\ldots,\bm{g}_{n})=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{\bm{g}_{i}}{\|\bm{g}_{i}\|_{2}}$ to properly average the normalized gradients. Without the 1/n factor, the aggregated vector's magnitude grows linearly with the number of workers, which contradicts the purpose of normalization to limit Byzantine influence.

comments.title: Attack formula assumes unrealistic omniscient knowledge
comments.quote:
019):

$$
\boldsymbol {v} := - \sum_ {i \in \text {g o o d}} \frac {\boldsymbol {g} _ {i}}{\| \boldsymbol {g} _ {i} \| _ {2}} \tag {6}
$$

O
comments.explanation: The normalized mean attack in Equation (6) requires Byzantine workers to know all good workers' normalized gradients simultaneously, but the paper provides no justification for how this information is obtained in federated learning settings where data remains private. The paper should either explain the attack's feasibility under realistic information constraints or explicitly state this is an omniscient attack model for theoretical analysis only.

comments.title: Resampling parameter inconsistency creates contradiction
comments.quote: Here we apply our proposed resampling with  $T = n$ ,  $s = 2$  to the same aggregation rules, showing that resampling overcomes the described failures.
comments.explanation: The text claims resampling uses T=n and s=2, but Algorithm 2's sampling without replacement constraint creates a contradiction. With n workers and s=2, we can only form n/2 unique pairs without replacement, not n pairs. To generate T=n resampled gradients with s=2, we would need 2n total selections, exceeding the available n workers. The authors should either clarify that sampling is done with replacement or adjust parameters to T=n/2 to match the algorithm's constraints.

comments.title: Variance formula derivation contains error
comments.explanation: The algebraic simplification in equation (11) is incorrect. Starting from the middle expression: (1/s)(1 - (s-1)/(sn-1))σ² = (1/s)((sn-1)/(sn-1) - (s-1)/(sn-1))σ² = (1/s)((sn-1-s+1)/(sn-1))σ² = (1/s)((s(n-1))/(sn-1))σ² = (n-1)/(sn-1)σ². While the final result happens to be correct, the intermediate step shows (1/s)(1 - (s-1)/(sn-1)) which doesn't match standard sampling without replacement formulas. The correct derivation should show the finite population correction factor directly: var(\bar{g}_t) = (σ²/s) * ((n-s)/(n-1)) = (n-s)σ²/(s(n-1)). This equals (n-1)/(sn-1)σ² only when s=1, suggesting the claimed variance reduction formula may be incorrect for s>1.

comments.title: Unfair MULTIKRUM comparison
comments.quote: Since resampling KRUM with  $s = 2$  actually averages 2 gradients, we compare it with MULTIKRUM with  $m = 2$ . The middle of Figure 2a shows that MULTIKRUM with  $m = 2$  performs better than KRUM, but KRUM with resampling is even better which suggests the resampling step improves the performance on non-iid data.
comments.explanation: The comparison is methodologically flawed. Resampled KRUM with s=2 first averages random pairs to create n new gradients, then selects one via KRUM. MULTIKRUM with m=2 first selects the 2 best original gradients via KRUM, then averages them. These operations differ fundamentally: resampling averages before selection (creating diverse candidates), while MULTIKRUM averages after selection (combining the best). The performance difference cannot be attributed solely to 'resampling step improves performance' when the selection mechanisms differ.

comments.title: Contradictory explanation of RFA robustness
comments.quote: Notice that RFA-T1 is more robust to the mimic attack than RFA-T8 in Figure 3 because more inner iterations lead to better approximate geometric median and less robust to normalized mean attacks.
comments.explanation: This explanation contains a logical contradiction. If more inner iterations lead to better approximation of the geometric median (which is the goal), then RFA-T8 should be more robust than RFA-T1, not less. The text claims RFA-T8 is 'less robust to normalized mean attacks' but this doesn't explain why it would be less robust to mimic attacks. The reasoning appears flawed - better approximation should improve robustness, not degrade it. Rewrite this sentence to correctly explain the observed phenomenon, or provide a clearer mechanistic explanation for why fewer iterations could paradoxically improve robustness against certain attacks.

comments.title: Dataset partitioning creates artificial heterogeneity
comments.quote: The dataset is sorted by labels and sequentially divided into equal parts among good workers; Byzantine workers have access to the dataset on all good workers.
comments.explanation: The sequential partitioning after label sorting creates an artificial worst-case heterogeneity where each worker sees only a few consecutive classes. This doesn't reflect realistic non-IID scenarios where workers typically have diverse but imbalanced data distributions. More importantly, the claim that Byzantine workers have access to the dataset on all good workers contradicts the federated learning premise where data remains private. This unrealistic assumption makes the attacks appear stronger than they would be in practice. The authors should either justify this omniscient adversary model or modify the experimental setup to reflect realistic information constraints.

comments.title: Definition A indexing notation error
comments.quote: Let $\mathcal{B}=\{j_{1},\ldots,j_{f}:j_{1}\leq j_{1}<\cdots<j_{f}\leq n\}$ be the indices of Byzantine workers.
comments.explanation: The condition $j_{1} \leq j_{1}$ is redundant and always true, failing to establish the required ordering of distinct indices. For a set of Byzantine worker indices, we need strictly increasing indices to ensure they are distinct and properly ordered. Rewrite the set definition as $\mathcal{B}=\{j_{1},\ldots,j_{f}:1 \leq j_{1} < j_{2} < \cdots < j_{f} \leq n\}$ to correctly specify distinct, ordered Byzantine worker indices.

comments.title: Undefined T parameter in RFA description
comments.quote: For example Rfa *(Pillutla et al., 2019)* with $T$=1 uses following aggregation rule:
comments.explanation: The parameter $T$ is introduced in the description of RFA but never defined or explained. The text states 'Rfa with $T$=1' without clarifying what $T$ represents or how it affects the aggregation rule. Since $T$ appears to be a key parameter of the RFA method (as evidenced by the reference to 'RFA with $T=8$' in Figure 1c), its definition should be provided when first mentioned.

comments.title: Undefined penalty term f₀ in RSA
comments.quote: where  $f_{0}$  is a strongly convex penalty term and  $\lambda > 0$  is a relaxation parameter.
comments.explanation: The penalty term $f_{0}$ is mentioned but never defined or explained. The text states it is 'a strongly convex penalty term' but does not specify its form, purpose, or relationship to the learning objective. Since $\nabla f_{0}(\bm{x}_{0})$ appears directly in the RSA aggregation rule (5), readers need to understand what $f_{0}$ represents to evaluate the method.

comments.title: Undefined variable in Algorithm 2 return statement
comments.quote: Return {g_t : t ∈ [T]}, {j_t^t : t ∈ [T], i ∈ [s]}
comments.explanation: The return statement references j_t^t which is never defined in the algorithm. The sampling loop uses j_i for indices, but there is no j_t^t variable. This appears to be a notation error for returning the sampling indices. Rewrite the return statement as: 'Return {g_t : t ∈ [T]}, {j_{t,i} : t ∈ [T], i ∈ [s]}' to match the j_i variables used in the sampling loop.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a resampling scheme to adapt Byzantine-robust aggregation to heterogeneous datasets. It demonstrates that existing methods fail against specific attacks and that resampling improves performance. However, the theoretical analysis contains a critical error in the convergence condition, the computational cost is mischaracterized as negligible, and the theoretical assumptions regarding gradient averaging in highly conflicting non-IID settings require clarification.

This work addresses a significant and timely problem: the failure of standard Byzantine defenses under realistic data heterogeneity. The resampling idea is simple and the empirical results are convincing. However, the theoretical contribution is undermined by a flawed convergence condition, and the practical assessment of computational overhead is misleading. The paper needs substantial revision to correct these fundamental issues before it can be accepted.

Overview issues:

- Incorrect Convergence Condition in Theorem II: Theorem II states a condition $2sf+3 \\leq n$ for convergence. However, the experiments in Section 6 use $n=10$ workers, $f=2$ Byzantine, and $s=2$. Substituting these values gives $2(2)(2)+3 = 11 \\leq 10$, which is false. The theorem would not apply to the experiments presented, yet the authors claim convergence. This suggests the derivation of the condition is incorrect or that the condition should be based on the number of resampled gradients $T$ rather than the original worker count $n$. The authors must re-derive the bound to ensure it holds for their experimental setup.

- Mischaracterization of Computational Overhead: The paper repeatedly claims the resampling step adds 'negligible computational cost' (Introduction, Remark 1). This is misleading. Algorithm 2 performs $T=n$ resampling iterations, each averaging $s$ gradients. For $n=1000$ workers and $s=2$, this requires $2000$ gradient additions per round. Standard Krum requires computing pairwise distances for all $n$ gradients ($O(n^2)$), while Resampling Krum requires $O(T \\cdot s \\cdot d)$ for averaging plus $O(T^2 \\cdot s^2)$ for the Krum selection. The overhead is substantial and scales linearly with $n$ and $T$. The authors should remove the 'negligible' claim and provide a concrete complexity analysis comparing the full pipeline (Resampling + Krum) to standard Krum.

- Narrow Scope of the Mimic Attack: Section 3.3 introduces the mimic2 attack to demonstrate the failure of geometric median methods. This attack requires that half the good workers have *identical* datasets and the other half have *another* identical dataset. While this proves a vulnerability, it is a very specific form of data heterogeneity that does not represent the general case. A robust defense should ideally handle arbitrary distributions, not just the case where good workers are perfectly split into two clusters. The paper should acknowledge that this attack targets a specific structural property of the data and discuss whether resampling provides robustness against more general, less structured non-IID data distributions.

- Assumption of Beneficial Gradient Averaging: The convergence proof (Theorem II) relies on Proposition I, which assumes the resampled gradients have mean $\\hat{\\mu}$ (Equation 9), effectively treating the average of good workers' gradients as the desirable direction. In non-IID federated learning, however, local optima can be vastly different. While the global optimum is the average of the local objectives, the gradients might be highly conflicting or orthogonal. The proof requires that the variance $\\sigma$ is bounded such that the condition $\\eta \\cdot \\sqrt{d} \\cdot \\sigma \\leq \\|\\nabla \\mathcal{L}\\| \\cdot \\sin \\alpha$ holds. The paper does not sufficiently discuss the regime of data heterogeneity where this condition fails, potentially leading to non-convergence or poor generalization even without attackers.

- Missing Analysis of Benign Non-IID Convergence: While Section 3.1 shows Krum fails on non-IID data without attackers and Section 6 shows resampling fixes this, the paper lacks a rigorous analysis of *why* resampling helps in this benign case. The variance reduction (Equation 8) is a statistical property, but the paper does not connect this to the convergence rate of SGD in non-IID settings. Specifically, does resampling reduce the 'gradient conflict' that slows down convergence in heterogeneous FL? A theoretical discussion or empirical demonstration of the convergence speedup (number of epochs to reach a target accuracy) would strengthen the contribution, showing that resampling is not just robust to attacks but also efficient for benign non-IID data.

Recommendation: Major Revision. The paper presents a promising idea with convincing empirical results, but the theoretical analysis contains a critical error in the convergence condition that invalidates the main theorem for the proposed experiments. Additionally, the claim of negligible computational cost is misleading. The authors must correct the mathematical derivation and provide a realistic assessment of the computational overhead before the work can be accepted at a top-tier venue.

Revision targets:

- Re-derive the convergence condition in Theorem II to correctly account for the number of resampled gradients $T$ and ensure it holds for the experimental setup ($n=10, f=2, s=2$).

- Provide a detailed computational complexity analysis for the Resampling + Krum pipeline and remove the misleading claim of 'negligible cost'.

- Discuss the limitations of the mimic attack regarding the requirement of two perfectly clustered datasets and clarify whether resampling is robust to arbitrary non-IID distributions.

- Add an analysis of the convergence speedup on benign non-IID data (without attackers) to demonstrate the efficiency benefit of resampling.

comments.title: Incorrect explanation of RFA robustness trade-off
comments.quote: Notice that RFA-T1 is more robust to the mimic attack than RFA-T8 in Figure 3 because more inner iterations lead to better approximate geometric median and less robust to normalized mean attacks.
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly states that RFA-T8 is less robust to normalized mean attacks; in fact, T8 approximates the geometric median, which is generally more robust to such attacks than the raw mean used in T1. Furthermore, the explanation regarding the mimic attack vulnerability is flawed because the geometric median approximation itself is the source of the mimic attack's success, not a lack of robustness to normalized means.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of computational overhead
comments.quote: which effectively adapts existing robust algorithms to heterogeneous datasets at a negligible computational cost.
comments.explanation: The claim of negligible computational cost contradicts the analysis of Algorithm 2, which requires $O(T 	imes s 	imes d)$ operations for averaging and $O(T^2 	imes s^2)$ for Krum selection. For large $n$ and $T$, these costs are substantial and should be quantified in a complexity comparison with standard Krum or FedAvg.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a resampling-based strategy to improve the performance of existing Byzantine-robust aggregation rules in non-IID federated learning settings. By averaging gradients via resampling, the method reduces local gradient variance, which the authors show is a primary failure mode for robust aggregators in heterogeneous environments.

The paper identifies a clear and relevant problem in federated learning: the sensitivity of robust aggregators to data heterogeneity. The proposed resampling mechanism is simple and effective, and the empirical results provide a convincing case for its utility. However, the theoretical analysis requires more rigor regarding the dependencies introduced by resampling.

Overview issues:

- Dependence of Resampled Gradients: Theorem II relies on the assumption that the resampled gradients are identically distributed, yet the paper acknowledges in Remark 2 that these gradients are not independent. Since most convergence proofs for Krum and other robust aggregators rely on the independence of the input vectors to bound the probability of selecting a Byzantine worker, this dependence is not trivial. The authors must demonstrate that the dependency structure introduced by the resampling (sampling without replacement from a finite set) does not invalidate the concentration bounds used in the proof of Theorem II. Providing a formal bound on the correlation between resampled gradients would significantly strengthen the theoretical claim.

- Sensitivity to Hyperparameter 's': The resampling parameter 's' represents a fundamental trade-off between variance reduction and the number of Byzantine-affected gradients. While the authors suggest that 's=2' is sufficient in practice, there is no formal analysis of how the choice of 's' interacts with the total number of Byzantine workers 'f' and the total workers 'n'. If 's' is chosen too large, the number of Byzantine-influenced resampled gradients could exceed the resilience threshold of the aggregation rule (e.g., 2sf + 3 <= n). A sensitivity analysis or a guideline for selecting 's' based on the estimated Byzantine fraction would make the method more robust for practitioners.

- Limited Scope of Theoretical Analysis: The paper restricts its formal convergence analysis to Krum, while the experimental section evaluates CM and RFA as well. Given that the failure modes for median-based methods (like RFA) are fundamentally different from distance-based methods (like Krum), the lack of theoretical justification for why resampling helps RFA is a gap. Extending the analysis to show that resampling reduces the bias introduced by the mimic attack on geometric median estimators would provide a more complete picture of why the method works across different aggregation families.

- Assumption of Known Byzantine Fraction: The algorithm assumes that the server knows 'f', the number of Byzantine workers, to perform the resampling correctly. In many real-world federated learning scenarios, the number of malicious participants is unknown or time-varying. The paper should address how the resampling strategy performs when the estimate of 'f' is inaccurate or if the server must adaptively estimate the level of corruption in the gradient pool.

- Absence of a formal trade-off analysis for the resampling parameter s: The paper suggests that s=2 is sufficient, but provides no formal analysis regarding the optimal selection of s relative to the Byzantine fraction f and the total number of workers n. While Remark 1 notes the trade-off between variance reduction and the risk of Byzantine contamination, the paper lacks a concrete guideline or heuristic for practitioners to tune s in environments with varying levels of corruption. To address this, the authors should provide a theoretical or empirical characterization of the 'breakdown point' for the Resampling-Krum combination as a function of s, f, and n, demonstrating the range of s values for which convergence is guaranteed.

- Missing theoretical justification for median-based aggregation rules: The theoretical convergence analysis is restricted to the Krum aggregation rule, despite the paper's empirical focus on median-based methods like CM and RFA. Because median-based methods rely on different geometric properties than Krum, the current proof does not explain why resampling mitigates the mimic attack for these rules. The paper would be more complete if it provided a brief theoretical sketch or a lemma showing how the resampling step transforms the distribution of gradients to satisfy the conditions required for the geometric median to remain robust under the mimic attack.

- Lack of robustness analysis for unknown Byzantine fraction f: The proposed resampling algorithm requires the server to know the number of Byzantine workers f to properly configure the resampling process. In realistic federated learning settings, this value is rarely known in advance. The paper lacks a discussion on whether the method remains effective when f is underestimated or how one might adaptively estimate f to set the resampling parameters. A simulation or a brief analytical discussion on the sensitivity of the performance to an incorrect estimate of f would provide necessary guidance for real-world deployment.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Address the dependency of resampled gradients in the proof of Theorem II by providing a formal bound on the correlation or using a concentration inequality that accounts for sampling without replacement.

- Include a brief sensitivity analysis of the parameter 's' to show how performance degrades as 's' approaches the theoretical limit imposed by the Byzantine resilience condition.

- Provide a discussion or heuristic on how to set 's' and 'f' in scenarios where the number of Byzantine workers is not known a priori.

comments.title: Ambiguity regarding the scope of theoretical validation
comments.quote: We theoretically and experimentally validate our approach, showing that combining resampling with existing robust algorithms is effective against challenging attacks.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims theoretical validation for 'existing robust algorithms' in general, yet Section 5 correctly notes that the formal convergence analysis is restricted to Krum. This creates a contradiction. Please revise the abstract to specify that the theoretical validation is focused on Krum-based aggregation, while the broader set of algorithms is supported empirically.

comments.title: Inaccurate description of Bulyan's resilience
comments.quote: imal algorithm for iid data. The distance-based approach of Krum was later extended in *Mhamdi et al. (2018)* who propose Bulyan to overcome the *dimensional leeway* attack. This is the so called *strong Byzantine resilience* and is orthogonal to the question of non-iid-ness we s
comments.explanation: The claim that Bulyan's resilience is orthogonal to non-IID data issues is misleading. Bulyan combines Krum with the Trimmed Mean, which relies on the assumption that gradients are centered around a common mean. In non-IID settings, the increased gradient variance can cause the Trimmed Mean component to discard legitimate gradients, reducing its robustness. Please acknowledge that Bulyan's performance is sensitive to the degree of non-IIDness.

comments.title: Misrepresentation of existing non-IID robust literature
comments.quote: far as we are aware, only *(Li et al., 2019; Ghosh et al., 2019; Sattler et al., 2020)* explicitly investigate Byzantine robustness with non-iid workers.
comments.explanation: This statement overlooks significant prior work, such as He, Karimireddy, and Jaggi (2020) in 'Byzantine-Robust Learning on Heterogeneous Datasets via Bucketing,' which directly addresses this problem using a bucketing scheme. Please include this work to correctly position your contribution.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Krum summation index definition
comments.quote: For $i\neq j$, let $i\rightarrow j$ denote that $\bm{g}_{j}$ belongs to the $n-f-2$ closest vectors to $\bm{g}_{i}$.
comments.explanation: The definition of the set i -> j is ambiguous because it does not explicitly exclude i from the set of neighbors. In the original Krum paper, the set is defined as the n-f-2 closest vectors to g_i excluding g_i itself. Please clarify that j != i for all j in the summation index set to ensure the cardinality of the sum is exactly n-f-2.

comments.title: Inconsistent naming of aggregation schemes
comments.quote: ii) schemes which use normalized means (e.g. Rsa *(Li et al., 2019)*), and iii) those which use the median (e.g. Rfa *(Pillutla et al., 2019)*). We show
comments.explanation: The acronyms used here are non-standard. The scheme by Li et al. (2019) is 'RSA', and the scheme by Pillutla et al. (2019) is 'RFA'. Using inconsistent casing creates unnecessary confusion for readers. Please update these to match the standard nomenclature in the cited literature.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper addresses the important problem of Byzantine-robust distributed learning under heterogeneous (non-iid) data. It identifies that existing robust aggregation rules fail under data heterogeneity, proposes a simple resampling scheme to adapt them, and provides theoretical convergence analysis for Krum combined with resampling. The empirical evaluation shows improvements across several attacks and aggregation methods.

The paper tackles a timely and practically relevant problem, and the proposed resampling scheme is simple and intuitive. The theoretical analysis, however, has significant gaps: the convergence proof relies on assumptions that are not fully justified for the heterogeneous setting, and the extension to other aggregation rules is claimed without proof. The experimental evaluation, while covering multiple attacks, is limited to a single dataset (MNIST) with a small MLP model, and lacks ablation studies on key hyperparameters like resampling size s and number of resampled gradients T. The paper's main contribution—showing that resampling enables existing robust methods to work on non-iid data—is promising but not fully substantiated.

Overview issues:

- Convergence proof for Krum with resampling has critical gaps: Theorem II claims that Krum ∘ Resampling is (α, sf)-Byzantine resilient and that gradients converge almost surely to zero. However, the proof (deferred to Appendix A) is not provided in the main text, and the assumptions raise concerns. Assumption (iv) requires η(T,sf)·√d·σ(x) ≤ ‖∇L(x)‖·sinα, where σ²(x) = (n-1)/(sn-1) σ̂²(x). This condition ties the allowed heterogeneity to the gradient norm, but σ̂²(x) is the variance of good worker gradients, which can be large under extreme non-iid data. The paper does not discuss when this condition holds or provide examples where it is satisfied. Moreover, the definition of (α,f)-Byzantine resilience (Definition A) assumes that the expected gradient of a randomly selected good worker equals the true gradient ∇L(x). This is a strong assumption that effectively requires that the server can sample uniformly from good workers, but the resampling procedure samples uniformly from all workers, including Byzantine ones. The proof must account for the fact that resampled gradients may be contaminated by Byzantine workers, and the condition 2sf+3 ≤ n is necessary but not sufficient to guarantee the resilience property. The paper should either provide a complete proof in the main text or clearly state the limitations of the theoretical result.

- Proposition 1's variance formula is questionable for the Byzantine setting: Proposition 1 states that for good resampled gradients, the variance is (n-1)/(sn-1) σ̂², where σ̂² is the variance of good worker gradients. This formula is derived from sampling theory assuming simple random sampling without replacement from a finite population. However, the resampling procedure in Algorithm 2 is not simple random sampling: it samples s gradients without replacement from the n workers, but the same worker can be sampled multiple times across the T resampled gradients (since c[i] is reset to 0 each outer loop? Actually the algorithm description is ambiguous—c[i] is initialized to 0 once, then incremented each time a worker is selected, and a worker is not selected if c[ji] ≥ s. This means each worker can appear at most s times across all T resampled gradients, which is a complex sampling design. The variance formula for a single resampled gradient under this design is not standard and needs derivation. Furthermore, the claim that 'at least T - sf gradients in {ḡ_t} are good' assumes that each Byzantine worker can contaminate at most s resampled gradients, but this is only true if Byzantine workers are identified and their gradients are excluded from resampling. The algorithm does not exclude any workers; it samples uniformly from all n workers. A Byzantine worker can be sampled multiple times, contaminating up to s resampled gradients, but the total number of contaminated gradients could be up to min(T, sf) if Byzantine workers are sampled repeatedly. The bound T - sf is an upper bound on the number of good gradients, not a lower bound. This error affects the theoretical guarantees.

- Experimental evaluation is insufficient to support the claims: The experiments are limited to a single dataset (MNIST) with a small MLP model. While the paper mentions additional results on Fashion-MNIST and CIFAR-10 in Appendix D, the main text only shows MNIST results. For a paper claiming to address realistic heterogeneous data, the evaluation should include multiple non-iid partitions (e.g., Dirichlet-based, label-skew with varying α) and larger-scale models (e.g., ResNet on CIFAR-10). The attacks tested are standard but the paper's own 'normalized mean' attack (Section 3.2) is only evaluated against RFA-T1, not against other aggregation rules. The mimic attack is tested only on median-based schemes. The paper should systematically evaluate all attacks against all aggregation rules with and without resampling. Additionally, there is no ablation study on the resampling hyperparameters s and T. The paper claims s=2 is sufficient, but this is only shown for one setting. The effect of varying s and T on accuracy and robustness should be explored. Finally, the paper does not compare against state-of-the-art methods designed for heterogeneous data, such as those in Li et al. (2019), Ghosh et al. (2019), or Sattler et al. (2020), which are mentioned in the related work. Without such comparisons, it is unclear whether resampling is competitive with existing approaches.

- The resampling scheme's computational cost is not analyzed: The paper claims the resampling scheme has 'negligible computational cost,' but this is not substantiated. Algorithm 2 involves sampling s gradients without replacement for each of T resampled gradients, which requires O(T·s) operations. If T = n (as in experiments), this is O(n·s) per iteration, which is comparable to the cost of computing gradients. However, the server must also run the robust aggregation rule (e.g., Krum) on the T resampled gradients, which may be more expensive than running it on the original n gradients if T > n. The paper uses T = n in experiments, so the cost is similar, but this should be explicitly discussed. More importantly, the resampling step requires the server to know which gradients are from which workers (to avoid sampling the same worker too many times), which may conflict with privacy-preserving techniques like secure aggregation. The paper acknowledges this briefly in the literature context but does not address it in the main text. The computational and communication overhead of resampling should be quantified and compared to baseline methods.

- The paper overclaims the generality of the resampling approach: The abstract and introduction claim that resampling 'adapts existing robust algorithms to heterogeneous datasets' and that the approach is effective 'against challenging attacks.' However, the theoretical analysis is only provided for Krum, and the experiments only test three aggregation rules (Krum, CM, RFA). The paper does not demonstrate that resampling works with other important robust aggregation rules like Trimmed Mean, Bulyan, or the method of Alistarh et al. (2018). The convergence proof for Krum relies on specific properties of Krum (distance-based selection) and may not generalize. The paper should either provide theoretical justification for other rules or clearly state that the theoretical guarantees are limited to Krum.
